crossref.abstract: <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:p>Bone morphogenetic proteins are essential for bone regeneration/fracture healing but can also induce heterotopic ossification (HO). Understanding accessory factors modulating BMP signaling would provide both a means of enhancing BMP-dependent regeneration while preventing HO. This study focuses on the ability of the collagen receptor, discoidin domain receptor 2 (DDR2), to regulate BMP activity. As will be shown, induction of bone formation by subcutaneous BMP2 implants is severely compromised in <jats:italic>Ddr2</jats:italic>-deficient mice. In addition, <jats:italic>Ddr2</jats:italic> deficiency attenuates HO in mice expressing the ACVR1 mutation associated with human fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva. In cells migrating into BMP2 implants, DDR2 is co-expressed with GLI1, a skeletal stem cell marker, and DDR2/GLI1-positive cells participate in BMP2-induced bone formation where they contribute to chondrogenic and osteogenic lineages. Consistent with this distribution, conditional knockout of <jats:italic>Ddr2</jats:italic> in <jats:italic>Gli1-</jats:italic>expressing cells inhibited bone formation to the same extent seen in globally <jats:italic>Ddr2</jats:italic>-deficient animals. This response was explained by selective inhibition of <jats:italic>Gli1</jats:italic><jats:sup>+</jats:sup> cell proliferation without changes in apoptosis. The basis for this DDR2 requirement was explored further using bone marrow stromal cells. Although <jats:italic>Ddr2</jats:italic> deficiency inhibited BMP2-dependent chondrocyte and osteoblast differentiation and in vivo, bone formation, early BMP responses including SMAD phosphorylation remained largely intact. Instead, <jats:italic>Ddr2</jats:italic> deficiency reduced the nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio of the Hippo pathway intermediates, YAP and TAZ. This suggests that DDR2 regulates Hippo pathway-mediated responses to the collagen matrix, which subsequently affect BMP responsiveness. In summary, DDR2 is an important modulator of BMP signaling and a potential therapeutic target both for enhancing regeneration and treating HO.</jats:p>
title: Discoidin domain receptor 2 is an important modulator of BMP signaling during heterotopic bone formation
